The company pays the vendor for 80 units based on the receiving document. Which data analytics test would be MOST likely to identify this short-shipment collusion scheme?
options: A. Comparison of the vendor's original sales order quantities and shipping manifests against the company's receiving reports to identify systematic patterns where received quantities are consistently lower than shipped quantities

B. Analysis of inventory turnover ratios by product category to identify items with unusually low turnover rates that might indicate excess inventory from unrecorded receipts accumulating in the warehouse
C. Review of vendor payment histories to detect instances where the same vendor receives multiple payments for identical invoice numbers or closely sequential invoice numbers on the same date

D. Examination of receiving dock supervisor approval patterns to identify any vendors for which the supervisor personally receives 100% of deliveries rather than sharing responsibility with other receiving personnel
questions.justification_correct: Comparing the vendor's shipping manifests (which would show 100 units shipped) against the receiving reports (which show only 80 units received) would reveal a systematic discrepancy. This gap between what the vendor claims to ship and what the company records as received would indicate either short-shipment fraud or receiving documentation fraud. The pattern would be especially suspicious if it occurs consistently with specific vendor-receiver combinations.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B (turnover analysis) would not detect this scheme because the unrecorded 20 units are removed from the facility entirely and never enter inventory, so there would be no excess accumulation affecting turnover ratios. Option C (duplicate payment analysis) is irrelevant because the company is only being invoiced and paying for the 80 units that were recorded, not for the diverted 20 units; there are no duplicate payments. Option D (approval pattern analysis) might show concentrated responsibility but does not identify the actual fraud; supervisors often have assigned vendor relationships, and receiving all deliveries from specific vendors may be legitimate.

questions.text: A store manager at a retail chain has been manipulating inventory shrinkage reports by intentionally miscounting high-value items during monthly cycle counts, reporting fewer units than actually exist. This creates a 'phantom shortage' in the system. The manager then steals the physical inventory equal to the phantom shortage over subsequent weeks. When the annual physical inventory occurs, actual counts match the system because the real theft equals the fabricated shortage. What characteristic of this scheme makes it particularly difficult to detect through traditional inventory controls?
options: A. The scheme self-reconciles during annual physical counts because the cumulative actual theft matches the cumulative fabricated shrinkage, creating no variance that would trigger investigation
B. The monthly cycle counts are conducted by the same individual who has custody of the inventory, eliminating the independent verification that would normally detect counting errors
C. The high-value items targeted by the perpetrator typically have lower inventory turnover rates, making unusual movement patterns more difficult to identify through sales trend analysis
D. The store manager has the authority to approve inventory adjustments resulting from cycle count variances without requiring regional management review or documentation of the business reason
questions.justification_correct: The scheme's defining characteristic is that it creates a self-reconciling fraud where fabricated shrinkage (phantom shortage) exactly offsets real theft. By the time an annual physical count occurs, the system balance equals the physical count because: System balance = (True beginning balance) - (Fabricated shortage) and Physical count = (True beginning balance) - (Actual theft matching fabricated shortage). This equilibrium makes variance-based detection methods ineffective because there is no variance to detect at the point of annual verification.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B (lack of segregation of duties) is a control weakness that enables the fraud but does not explain why it is particularly difficult to detect; many frauds involve the same person performing incompatible functions and are still detected through other means like variance analysis. Option C (turnover rates) is not the distinguishing factor making this scheme hard to detect; high-value items with low turnover are often more closely monitored, and this does not address the self-reconciling nature of the fraud. Option D (adjustment approval authority) is an enabling control weakness but does not explain the detection difficulty; the fundamental issue is that annual counts show no variance, regardless of who approved interim adjustments.

questions.text: A production scheduler at a semiconductor manufacturer has been creating false 'engineering sample' requests for custom-designed processors that are actually sold to unauthorized distributors. The production system shows these units as scrapped after quality testing failure. The company's procedures require engineering samples to undergo destructive testing. Financial impact is estimated at $890,000 over two years. Which investigative finding would provide the MOST direct evidence that the engineering samples were diverted rather than destroyed?
options: A. Documentation showing that the number of engineering sample units requested significantly exceeds the quantity necessary for the stated testing protocols based on standard statistical sampling methodologies
B. Forensic imaging of production equipment demonstrating that unique processor serial numbers recorded as scrapped are appearing in online marketplaces or authorized distributor databases
C. Testimony from quality assurance personnel indicating they did not receive the engineering samples for destructive testing and have no documentation of test results for the units in question
D. Financial analysis revealing that the production scheduler made bank deposits totaling $240,000 during the period when engineering samples were requested, with no apparent legitimate source
questions.justification_correct: Forensic evidence showing that specific serialized processors that were documented as scrapped are actually circulating in commercial channels provides direct, objective proof that the units were diverted rather than destroyed. Serial numbers create a definitive link between the units claimed to be scrapped and physical products in the market, directly contradicting the documented disposition and proving the fraud occurred. This is physical evidence of the misappropriation itself.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A (excessive sample quantities) provides circumstantial evidence suggesting over-ordering but does not directly prove diversion occurred; excess samples could theoretically be justified by expanded testing needs or legitimately scrapped. Option C (quality assurance testimony) shows a process breakdown and lack of testing documentation but does not affirmatively prove the units were diverted; they could have been scrapped through another undocumented process. Option D (financial analysis) provides evidence of potential proceeds but is circumstantial regarding the source of funds and does not directly connect the engineering samples to the deposits or prove diversion occurred.

questions.text: An inventory control specialist at a medical device distributor has been exploiting the company's consignment inventory program. The specialist creates false 'consignment consumption' reports claiming that hospitals used devices that were never actually implanted. The specialist removes the unreported devices from consignment locations and sells them. The company bills hospitals for the falsely reported consumption and writes off actual missing inventory as 'unexplained consignment shrinkage.' Which reconciliation control would be MOST effective in detecting this fraud scheme?
options: A. Requiring hospitals to provide independent confirmation of consignment inventory consumption by submitting copies of patient procedure records or implant logs that corroborate the quantities reported as used
B. Implementing monthly physical counts of consignment inventory at hospital locations conducted by the inventory control specialist with sign-off by hospital materials management personnel
C. Establishing a variance analysis procedure that compares consignment shrinkage rates across hospital locations and investigates any location exceeding 3% shrinkage quarterly
D. Mandating that all consignment consumption reports be approved by the inventory control specialist's supervisor before billing is processed and revenue is recognized
questions.justification_correct: Independent hospital confirmation using patient procedure records or implant logs provides third-party verification of actual device usage that is independent of the perpetrator's consumption reports. Hospitals would not confirm consumption of devices that were never implanted in patients, creating an investigable discrepancy between the specialist's false reports and the hospital's records. This control introduces an external verification that the fraudster cannot manipulate.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B (physical counts by the specialist) is ineffective because the perpetrator conducting the counts can manipulate them to conceal the missing inventory; having hospital personnel sign off adds minimal value if they are not independently verifying against their own usage records. Option C (shrinkage variance analysis) might eventually identify locations with high shrinkage, but the fraud describes writing off the actual shrinkage, so this may be dismissed as normal consignment shrinkage patterns rather than triggering fraud investigation. Option D (supervisor approval) relies on reviewing the specialist's reports without independent verification of actual consumption; the supervisor has no basis to question reports that appear facially reasonable unless there is external corroboration.
